data.title: Google looks to help publishers amid growing AI search worries - The Information
data.description:
Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) is hiring new staff to market its publisher advertising technology to big advertisers and ad agencies, according to a report by The Information on Thursday, citing two publishing executives.

The move comes after recent media reports suggested that traffic on publishers' websites has slowed due to the company's AI-powered search results.

A spokesperson for the tech giant told The Information that the company had been investing for years in its business to help publishers sell ads and grow.

“Like the rest of the industry, we’re also focused on helping publishers better connect with agencies directly,” the spokesperson reportedly said.

Publishers get "some of the lowest prices on the ad space" sold by Google Ad Manager when compared to rival ad tech firms like PubMatic (PUBM) and Magnite (MGNI), two publishers told The Info.

That's in part because mostly small businesses buy ads on publisher sites through Google’s tools, and they’re less likely to pay a premium for ad space, they said.

In a push to put more resources into publisher tools, Google launched "Offerwall" last week, which allows readers different ways of paying for content, such as paying directly or watching an ad before reading an article.

data.title: Riot sees 76% Y/Y rise in June bitcoin production
data.description:
Riot Platforms (NASDAQ:RIOT) said on Thursday that it produced 450 bitcoins in June, down 12% from the prior month but 76% higher year-over-year.

Average bitcoins produced per day was 15 in June.

The company held 19,273 bitcoins as of June-end, and sold 397 bitcoins for net proceeds of $41.7M the previous month.

Average net price per bitcoin sold came at $105,071. BTC (BTC-USD) was trading at $109,919 during morning trade.

The deployed hash rate for June was 35.5 EH/s, while the average operating hash rate was 29.8 EH/s.

data.title: Carlyle to offload up to 10% stake in India's Piramal Pharma - report
data.description:
Carlyle Group (NASDAQ:CG) is set to sell up to a 10% stake in Indian drugmaker Piramal Pharma, in a deal that could fetch the U.S.-based private equity firm 26B-27B rupees ($304.3M-$316.0M), Moneycontrol reported on Thursday, citing sources.

Carlyle (NASDAQ:CG) bought a 20% stake in Piramal in 2020 for around $490M, the article said.

CG shares gained 2.2% in late morning trading.

data.title: Hargreaves Lansdown’s three stocks for the long term
data.description:
U.S. stocks (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) have rebounded strongly after a challenging start, as investors have now shifted their focus from tariff concerns to potential earlier-than-expected rate cuts and easing tensions in the Middle East, said Derren Nathan, head of equity research at Hargreaves Lansdown.

While the “Magnificent Seven” tech giants (GOOGL), (AMZN), (AAPL), (META), (MSFT), (NVDA), (TSLA) continue to dominate headlines, “there’s plenty of quality on the U.S. exchanges,” he said, and pointed out three stocks for the long-term:

Intuitive Surgical (NASDAQ:ISRG) stands out as a promising long-term investment with its revolutionary robot-assisted surgical platform delivering “huge benefits to patients, surgeons and healthcare providers,” Nathan said.

The company has achieved an average revenue growth of 14.6% over the past decade, with only a single down year in 2020 due to Covid-19’s impact on health systems. Also, while the company trades on a premium multiple, successful product rollouts could potentially drive “significant upside to market forecasts,” he said.

Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) has emerged as “a formidable force in cloud computing” alongside its established leadership in data storage and business software, Nathan added. Its rising prominence was recently underscored by a substantial $30B deal with OpenAI, further enhancing its already substantial revenue backlog of $138B.

Oracle’s (NYSE:ORCL) competitive advantage lies in how “smoothly it connects its powerful data storage with flexible cloud services,” allowing it to compete effectively against tech giants like Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOGL), and Microsoft (MSFT).

Finally, Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT), with its nearly century-long history of manufacturing “mission-critical heavy machinery,” presents another compelling investment opportunity despite recent challenges from “weak construction activity,” Nathan said.

The company is well-positioned to benefit from “ambitious infrastructure plans in the U.S.” and increasing “demand for minerals critical to the energy transition,” which should boost its mining equipment sales. Furthermore, “the AI boom not only presents an opportunity to benefit from data center construction,” but also drives demand for Caterpillar’s power generation products, Nathan said.

Lastly, while Caterpillar’s (NYSE:CAT) share price “has recovered strongly of late,” potential investors might consider it “one to buy on the dips” given possible “short-term challenges ahead.” This tactical approach reflects the broader market sentiment that despite current volatility, companies with strong fundamentals and alignment with major technological and infrastructure trends remain attractive for long-term investment strategies.

data.title: U.S. natural gas inventory net change of +55 Bcf for week ended June 27
data.description:
• Stocks for the week ended June 27 were 2,953 Bcf vs. 2,898 Bcf for the week ended June 20.

data.title: Intercontinental Exchange sees 21% Y/Y rise in June average daily volume
data.description:
Intercontinental Exchange (NYSE:ICE) announced on Thursday a 21% year-over-year increase in June average daily volume, with open interest up 9% from last year.

For the second quarter, the ADV was up 26% from the prior year.

Energy ADV rose 28% on a yearly basis in June. Financials ADV was up 14%, NYSE Cash Equities ADV 43%, and NYSE Equity Options ADV increased 2%.

data.title: UPS offers buyout to union drivers, drawing ire from Teamsters
data.description:
United Parcel Service (NYSE:UPS) will reportedly offer its union drivers a severance offer as part of the company’s “network reconfiguration."

The package offered through its “Driver Voluntary Severance Plan” is in addition to any retirement benefits, including health care and pension disbursements, according to a company emailed cited by Bloomberg.

But according to the Teamsters union -- which represents 340,000 UPS (NYSE:UPS) drivers -- the “illegal” plan violates the contract between UPS and the Teamsters signed in August 2023, and would be less than what rank-and-file Teamsters now earn and could continue to make over the life of the current agreement.

Under the August 2023 agreement, UPS (NYSE:UPS) agreed to create 22,500 more full-time jobs with existing part-time employees and create at least 7,500 new full-time positions, a commitment the Teamsters union claims UPS (UPS) is reneging on by cutting its workforce through voluntary severance.

“UPS is obligated to establish tens of thousands of new full-time jobs under the agreement. But CEO Carol Tomé and UPS’s corporate managers are hoping that if they offer palty severance packages, no one will notice the company is setting the union’s contract on fire,” Teamsters president Sean O’Brien said in a statement.

The 2023 contract gave pay raises to both part-time and full-time drivers, boosting the average pay of full-time drivers to $49/hour. Current workers would get $2.75 more an hour the first year and $7.50 more an hour over the term of the five-year contract, wage increases the union acknowledged were “overwhelmingly lucrative.”

While UPS (UPS) said most of the costs of the contract are “front-loaded,” the deal increases wage and benefit costs at a 3.3% compound annual growth rate over the lifetime of the agreement.

Less than a year after the contract was signed, UPS (UPS) cut 12K jobs, attributed to higher union labor costs.

Besides higher wage costs during a time of economic uncertainty, the buyout offer comes as UPS (UPS) is scaling back Amazon (AMZN) deliveries to concentrate on more profitable volume. As part of this change, UPS (UPS) initiated its largest reconfiguration in the company’s history through “capacity optimization measures” that includes increased automation.

“With this reconfiguration, we will also lessen our dependency on labor, reduce the capital requirements needed to run the network, and will drive structural operating margin and return on invested capital improvements,” CEO Carol Tomé said in April on the company’s earnings call.

Since the August 2023 agreement with the Teamsters union, UPS (UPS) shares have eroded by 42%.

data.title: U.S. PMI Composite revised slightly higher in final June reading
data.description:
The S&P Global U.S. Composite PMI final print for June edged up to 52.9, compared with the advance reading of 52.8, but the month ticked down from 53.0 in May, according to data released on Thursday.

"Growth of the US private sector economy remained solid in June, and little changed since May," S&P Global said.

The Services PMI print settled to 52.9 vs. initial print of 53.1 and 53.7 in May.

"The US service sector reported a welcome combination of sustained growth and increased hiring in June, but also reported elevated price pressures, all of which could add to pressure on policymakers to remain cautious with regard to any further loosening of monetary policy,"  said Chris Williamson, Chief Business Economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence.

data.title: Exxon-Qatar JV seeks U.S. permission to re-export LNG from Golden Pass
data.description:
The Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM) and QatarEnergy Golden Pass LNG joint venture has asked U.S. regulators for permission to re-export liquefied natural gas from October 1, Reuters reported late Wednesday, as the export plant nears production after previous delays.

The JV said it wants to re-export a cargo of LNG that it plans to import to cool down its export plant in Texas, which is still under construction; cooling down the plant is a standard procedure typically required before full-scale LNG production begins.

Golden Pass is building an 18M metric tons/year LNG export facility in Sabine Pass, Texas, and plans to start exporting LNG later this year, but the project is behind schedule and over budget after a series of problems.

The start-up was thrown into doubt recently when lead contractor Zachry Holdings filed for bankruptcy and quit the project, which was 75% complete.

Following Zachry's exit, McDermott International (OTC:MCDIF) was appointed as lead contractor for Golden Pass LNG Train 1 and is negotiating to assume responsibility for the remaining two trains at the facility.

data.title: Factory orders rise more than expected in May
data.description:
May Factory Orders: +8.2% M/M vs. +8.1% consensus and  -3.9% prior (revised from -3.7%).

Excluding transportation: +0.2% M/M vs. -0.6% prior (revised from -0.5%).
